rationale: This reaction shows the methylenation of a simple ketone, acetone, to form 2-methylpropene. This is a classic textbook example of the Tebbe olefination, converting a C=O bond into a C=CH2 bond using the Tebbe reagent. The reactants and products are small, acyclic, and clearly illustrate the core transformation without introducing confounding functional groups.
collision_avoidance_note: This example uses a simple aliphatic ketone to avoid overlap with reactions involving aromatic rings (e.g., Friedel-Crafts), esters (e.g., Claisen condensation), or more complex carbonyl compounds. It is distinct from the Wittig reaction by the nature of the reagent (titanocene-based vs. phosphorus ylide) and its broader substrate scope (e.g., esters), although this simple example focuses on the most common ketone transformation.
